title: Beyond Chatbots: Building a Multi-Agent Research-to-Code Pipeline with LangGraph
author: Xyzvilan
text:
Beyond Chatbots: Building a Multi-Agent Research-to-Code Pipeline with LangGraph
Stop prompting. Start orchestrating. Here’s how production agentic AI systems actually work — with real, runnable Python code you can use today.
The chatbot era is officially over.
In 2026, the most capable AI systems don’t just respond — they research, plan, write code, and self-correct, all without you lifting a finger. If you’re still manually copying AI output into your IDE, you’re already behind the teams shipping agentic pipelines in production.
In this tutorial, you’ll build a complete Multi-Agent Research-to-Code Pipeline using LangGraph. Three specialized AI agents — a Researcher, an Architect, and an Auditor — collaborate inside a cyclic graph to produce clean, tested Python code autonomously.
By the end, you’ll have a working system that:
- Researches the best approach for any coding task
- Writes clean, type-hinted Python code based on that research
- Audits the output for quality — and loops back to retry if it falls short
- Deploys only when the code actually passes the quality gate
Let’s build it.
Why Agentic Workflows Beat Standard LLM Calls
Standard LLM calls are “one-shot” — you send a prompt, you get one response. That works fine for simple questions. It breaks down fast for anything requiring multi-step logic, research, or error recovery.
Agentic workflows solve this with three core principles:
Specialization — Each agent is narrowly scoped. One searches documentation, another designs the code structure, a third enforces quality standards. Specialists consistently outperform generalists on complex tasks.
Iteration — Agents can loop back when output doesn’t pass a quality gate. Instead of accepting a mediocre first attempt, the pipeline keeps refining until the bar is met.
Structured reasoning — Breaking a big task into smaller steps dramatically reduces hallucination. Teams building production agentic systems report roughly 40% fewer hallucinations on complex coding tasks compared to single-shot prompting — because a dedicated Auditor catches failures before they ever reach the user.
The Architecture: The “Trio” Pattern
This pipeline uses three nodes wired into a cyclic state graph:
Researcher → Architect → Auditor
|
PASS → Deploy (END)
FAIL → back to Researcher ↩
The Researcher scans documentation and identifies the best libraries and patterns for the task.
The Architect takes those research notes and writes clean, type-hinted Python code based on them.
The Auditor checks the output against quality standards and routes execution: deploy if it passes, retry if it doesn’t.
LangGraph is the right tool here because it supports cyclic graphs — unlike linear chains, it lets the Auditor send execution back to the Researcher for another pass. That retry loop is the entire point.
The Implementation
Step 1 — Define the Shared State
Every agent reads from and writes to a shared AgentState
object. This is the single source of truth for the entire pipeline run.
python
from typing import TypedDict
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
class AgentState(TypedDict):
task: str # The user's original request
research_notes: str # Output from the Researcher
code_output: str # Output from the Architect
is_clean: bool # Audit result flag
iterations: int # Safety counter to prevent infinite loops
Step 2 — Define the Agent Nodes
Each function is a node in the graph. In a production system these would call real LLMs or search tools. Here they’re simplified so the structure stays crystal clear.
python
def researcher_agent(state: AgentState):
print("Step 1: Researching best practices...")
# In production: call a search tool like Tavily here
notes = f"Research for '{state['task']}': Use modular functions and type hints."
return {
"research_notes": notes,
"iterations": state.get("iterations", 0) + 1
}
def architect_agent(state: AgentState):
print("Step 2: Writing clean Python code...")
# In production: call an LLM with research_notes as context
code = (
f"# Task: {state['task']}\n"
"def execute_task() -> str:\n"
" \"\"\"Execute the defined task and return a status.\"\"\"\n"
" return 'Success'"
)
return {"code_output": code}
def auditor_agent(state: AgentState) -> str:
print("Step 3: Auditing for clean code standards...")
# Gate 1: must contain a proper function definition
# Gate 2: cap retries at 3 to prevent infinite loops
if "def " in state["code_output"] and state.get("iterations", 0) < 3:
return "deploy"
return "retry"
Pro tip: Always add an
iterations
counter with a max-retry gate in your auditor. Without it, a persistent failure will cause the graph to loop forever and drain your token budget.
Step 3 — Orchestrate the Graph
This is where LangGraph earns its place. The add_conditional_edges
call is what enables the retry loop — the Auditor's return value determines the next node.
python
# Initialize the graph with our shared state schema
workflow = StateGraph(AgentState)
# Register the nodes
workflow.add_node("researcher", researcher_agent)
workflow.add_node("architect", architect_agent)# Set the entry point
workflow.set_entry_point("researcher")# Linear edge: researcher → architect
workflow.add_edge("researcher", "architect")# Conditional edge: architect → deploy or retry (this is the cyclic part)
workflow.add_conditional_edges(
"architect",
auditor_agent, # This function decides the route
{
"deploy": END, # Code passed — pipeline done
"retry": "researcher" # Code failed — loop back
}
)# Compile and run
app = workflow.compile()
result = app.invoke({"task": "Build a REST API client", "iterations": 0})
print(result["code_output"])
Run this and you’ll see the agents execute in sequence, with the Auditor making the final call on whether to ship or retry.
Why This Architecture Scales
The Trio Pattern isn’t just a tutorial toy — it’s the foundation of how serious agentic systems are built. Once you have this working, scaling it up is straightforward:
- Replace the simulated Researcher with Tavily or another real search API
- Wire the Architect node to an actual LLM call via LangChain’s ChatAnthropic or ChatOpenAI
- Add a memory layer so the pipeline learns from past failures within a session
- Extend with more nodes: a Security Auditor, a Documentation Writer, a Test Generator
Same graph structure, just more nodes. You’re not adding complexity — you’re adding capability.
The Real Takeaway
A developer’s most valuable skill in 2026 isn’t writing code.
It’s designing the systems that write it.
Agentic orchestration is that skill. Your job is shifting from individual contributor to architect — you define the workflow, the quality gates, and the handoffs. The agents do the execution.
The Trio Pattern you just built is the foundation. Everything else is just adding more specialists to the team.
What are you building with agentic AI this year? Drop a comment — I read every one.
